content:
Redd had commandeered a library table that was technically meant for four people and practically meant for two plus a reckless number of books. Several tomes were stacked in uneven towers around him—fiend classifications, historical incidents in the Obsidian Barricade, one that looked suspiciously like it hadn’t been opened past the table of contents. Notes were scattered between them like casualties.

He glanced up at Auri, then immediately let out a deep sigh.

“Okay. Thanks again for doing this with me,” he said, lowering his voice out of habit rather than necessity. “Research is… not my strongest survival instinct.”
He gestured vaguely at the papers in front of him, one of which had a very judgmental ink blot.

“I did spectacularly bad on the written portion of my midterms. Like—impressively so.” A beat. “Thank every god listening that the practical was half the grade, or Animal and Nature Studies would’ve taken me out back, and that would have been incredibly embarrassing for a Druid”

Redd leaned back in his chair, balancing it briefly on two legs before letting it thump back down.

“Bs get degrees and all.” He winced theatrically. “C in Poli Sci, though. Apparently ‘strong opinions delivered confidently’ is not the same thing as knowing what I’m talking about.”

He tilted his head toward the pile labeled Kalen Fiends, squinting at a paragraph like it might flinch first.

“So. Yes. I am very grateful you’re here to stop me from confidently writing something deeply wrong.” A crooked grin followed. “Please interrupt me at any point if I start inventing facts.”

((<@697171541208596510> ))

Auri glanced over the table, eyes flicking from the leaning tower of books to the scattered notes, then back to him. One corner of her mouth lifted. 

“Of course,” she said quietly, matching his volume as she slid into the chair across from Redd, “Research is one of my most beloved hobbies.” She set her bag down and reached out to steady one of the more precarious stacks before it could betray him. Her mouth twitched, then she sighed softly and leaned back. 

“I can’t judge you, really. I took some time off too during the break.” She winced slightly. “I got a B in Government. I also helped Vornakir with his work in that class when he was out— and he got a C, so I’m not sure how much I actually helped.” The words came out like a confession. “Which I *know* it’s all objectively fine,” she added, rubbing at her temple, “but…I am not over it.” 

She planted her elbows on the table, resting her chin in her hands, and shook her head once. “So now,” she continued, glancing at him with a wry expression. “You don’t get to spiral alone. We can be disappointed *together*.”

Auri pulled one of the tomes at random and opened it. It was, unsurprisingly, also about fiends. “But,” she said lightly, “I’m here. Think of me as your scholarly impulse control.” She skimmed a paragraph, then looked back up at him. “Alright,” she said. “Show me what you’re working on.” He teased her, causing her to playfully roll her eyes and grin. “Please, you know I’ll have no qualms about interrupting you if you start inventing facts.”

Redd flipped one of the heavier tomes around and squinted at a passage he’d already underlined twice, then cleared his throat and read aloud anyway.

“‘*Kalen Fiends originating from the Obsidian Barricade exhibit patterns consistent with external direction, suggesting sustained control by an unidentified intelligence rather than independent volition.*’”

He stopped, stared at the page, then let his forehead dip forward until it lightly thunked against the book.

“Great,” he muttered. “So they’re dangerous, summoned, and being puppeted by someone we still can’t name. Riveting. Truly. I love learning the same thing five different ways.”

Redd dragged the book back into its stack with a sigh, fingers raking briefly through his hair as if he could physically shake a new insight loose. “I swear, if one more scholar or archmage says ‘unidentified intelligence’, I’m going to throw up.”

*As he reached for another volume, his hand paused.

His eyes caught on a different spine wedged between two far more ominous-looking texts. The title was unmistakable.*

**A Field Guide to Drow Courtship (Revised Edition)**.

Redd blinked once. Then the corner of his mouth curved, slow and unmistakably pleased.

“Well,” he said, tone immediately lighter, tapping the spine with one finger, “this is suddenly a much more interesting section of the Obsidian Barricade.”

He glanced up at Auri, brows lifting innocently. “Technically,” he added, “it’s still regional research.” A beat. “And given your… current extracurricular associations with a certain drow from that region, I’d argue this counts as being academically responsible.”

Redd slid the book partway out, glancing down at it like an old acquaintance. “Revised edition, too,” he noted. “Always good when a culture updates its expectations.”

He tipped his head, smirk deepening. “Tell you what—before I lose the will to live to another paragraph about fiends being ‘mysterious,’ we take a quick break. Purely educational.” A pause, then, casually: “I want to see your reaction when you read some of this shit.”

That earned him a sideways look of his own making, and he grinned wider.

“Trust me,” he said lightly. “This one’s going to be relevant.”

Auri huffed a laugh and pointed to the words in the tome spread open before her. “Listen to this one— ‘*Patterns exhibited by Kalen Fiends are inconsistent with self-determined behavior, instead supporting the hypothesis of enduring control exerted by an…*’” She paused, lips quirking. “You know the rest. I have no desire to make you throw up in one of my favorite places on campus. Sol already blew up one of the shelves.” 

Her tail curled into a question mark when Redd went quiet. Curious, she leaned forward to see what had caught his attention, especially when his mouth curved with unmistakable mischief. The second she registered the title, she immediately leaned back into her chair and shook her head. 

“Absolutely not.” Reaching across the table, she nudged the book into one of the surrounding stacks. “We’ve been on *one* date. And while I—” she cleared her throat, cheeks warming as her gaze softened into something distinctly dreamy, “--- really, *really* like him…”

After her date with Vornakir had been confirmed as a *date*, she’d gone straight to Redd. She hadn’t shared anything she thought Vornakir would want kept private, but she hadn’t spared the rest, least of all how hopelessly taken she already was. 

 “--- I am not about to start preemptively researching courtship rituals like I’m cramming for an exam I haven’t been assigned yet.”

Her voice softened, fondness curling into her smile. “If Vornakir wants me to know about drow courting traditions, I want to hear it from *him*, not from a *Revised Edition*. I do trust you, but that’s the right thing to do, isnt it?”

With a small shake of her head, she slid one of the fiend tomes back toward him. “Right now,” Auri said, a spark of focus returning, “I know I said I wanted to read about where he’s from, but I meant reading about things like the ecosystem, natural wildlife, political structure— things like that.”

She met his eyes again. “Nice try, but we have to study.”

Redd watched the book disappear into the stack, lips pressed together like he was honoring the decision.

Then he exhaled through his nose and tipped his head back slightly.

“Come oooon,” he said, making it clear he had absolutely no intention of letting this go.

He leaned forward again, resting his forearms on the table, eyes flicking pointedly toward the pile where the book now hid. “I hear everything you’re saying,” he continued, tone earnest in a way that was very obviously insincere. “This is not about Vornakir. This is about the Obsidian Barricade. Regional context. Cultural literacy. Very important. Completely unrelated to the fact that your… hypothetical interest happens to be a drow from there.”

A pause. A grin.

“And anyway,” Redd added, “you’re the one who said reading counts as 'acceptable leisure'. This is reading.” He held up a finger. “Five minutes. No towns or schools in danger. We’ll go right back to fiends and their mysterious, ever-unidentified puppet masters after. I promise.”

He reached over and nudged the book back into the open space between them, deliberately stopping short of opening it himself. “Besides,” he said wiggling his eyebrows, “don't you want to know what they do with the masks and cords??"

At her look—Redd’s grin widened. “Nope. Not explaining.” He leaned back in his chair, folding his arms with exaggerated patience. “I could tell you. Or you could read it.”

He lifted his brows, clearly enjoying himself. “Five minutes,” he repeated.

Auri stared at the book, then at Redd, then back at the book. “Okay, okay…you’re right. I *did* say that.” She closed her eyes and pinched the bridge of her nose, exhaling. “...Fine, *five minutes*,” she said, removing the book from the stack and drawing it toward her. “Exactly five. I’m only doing this because you clearly know something, and you’re probably going to keep asking until the sun burns out.” 

She flipped the book open, braced for something scandalous based on Redd’s insistence and ominous mentions of cords and masks, and instead blinked. Her brows knit as she skimmed the first section, lips moving silently. “...*The Veiled Intent*,” she murmured. “An exchange of sigils…worn publicly and proudly…” She tilted her head, scanned again, then glanced up at Redd, genuinely confused. “That’s just…” She searched for the word, then smiled softly. “Sweet.”

Unbidden, the image surfaced: the bracelet she’d given Vornakir. How he’d worn it ever since. Auri swallowed. “I didn’t know,” she admitted quietly. “I gave him a bracelet without realizing it might mean something more. I wanted it to be a reminder that he isn’t alone.” Her thumb brushed the page absently. “But he wears it,” she added, a fond, distant look crossing her face. “All the time.” 

She shook her head, as if clearing it, and flipped the page. Then she froze. “...*The Draught of Devotion*,” she read. Her eyes widened. She reread the paragraph, then read it again. Slowly, she lifted her gaze to Redd. “...They *what*?” She blinked. “They drink *poison*,” she said, incredulous. “On *purpose*.” Her mouth opened, then closed. She picked the book up, scanning again. “It’s forbidden to seek the antidote unless you intend to drink it…and you trust that the other person will be there to take it with you.” A beat. “...Okay. Don’t panic,” she said, more to herself than anyone else. She leaned back in her chair, the book still open but forgotten for the moment, staring at nothing. Her tail curled around her legs as she stared out at the stacks of books, lost in thought.

Finally, she spoke. “So courting isn’t just dating,” she said slowly. “It’s declaring intent.Trusting someone with your life, and choosing to share it with that person knowing what it costs.”  Her grip on the book tightened just a little. “I think,” she said carefully, knowing that what she was about to say scared her, “I could want that with him, but…there are things I’d need to talk about with him first. Big things, like…our families, the differences between our lives, my goals. Whether our lives fit together outside of feelings. And, of course, he’d have actually ask me first.”

“Five minutes are up,” she declared. She closed the book gently and slid it back toward Redd. “I’m glad I trusted you,” she added. “You were right. I’m glad I read this. Even if Vornakir explained all of this himself before asking me, I probably would have agreed based on how I feel alone, and that wouldn’t be wise.”

Auri rested her head against her hand, elbow on the table, and sighed. “Gods…I’m crazy about him, and I *do* trust him that much.” She faltered, then huffed a quiet laugh. “Please be honest with me, Redd. Am I insane for seriously considering this? Why am I even considering it?” A small, rueful breath. “I like to think I’m a scholarly, logical, level-headed woman…and this doesn’t feel logical *at all*.”

Redd let out a soft breath that might’ve been a laugh as he reached for his quill again, rolling it between his fingers while she spoke.

“Alright,” he said, eyes dropping briefly to his notes as if consulting them for a diagnosis. “If you’re insane, it’s doing a very bad job of showing.” He glanced back up at her. “You read all that and immediately started weighing intent, consent, consequences, and long-term compatibility. That’s not madness. That’s… deeply on brand.”

He dipped the quill and added a short line to the margin, then hesitated, as if the thought hadn’t quite settled yet. “I’m… not completely sure,” he said, glancing back at her, “but that does actually sound like Stage One. Token given, worn publicly, intent acknowledged—even if neither of you framed it as a ritual at the time.”

He shrugged, thoughtful rather than certain. “You didn’t set out to perform it, but the structure’s there.” He tapped the quill once against the table. “Sometimes the ritual works even when the people involved aren’t trying to invoke it. Although you may wanna have a conversation with Vornakir about it.”

He flipped a page, scanning a paragraph, then spoke again, tone still easy. “For what it’s worth,” he said, “Vornakir and I did talk about you.” He glanced up quickly, reading her expression. “I'm not gonna go into specifics that were said in confidence.” 

Redd underlined a sentence, the scratch of the quill soft in the quiet. “But was very clear about one thing,” he continued. “That it would be something he may choose to go through *with* you. And that he would never put you in danger.” 

He met her eyes again. “So talk to him. He's not the type to make you do things you'll be uncomforable with” His voice softened. “Those rituals are meant to force honesty before commitment.”

He capped the quill, nudged the fiend tome back into place, and let the moment settle before adding, gently, “You’re considering it because it matters. And you’re being careful because you matter. Those two things can exist at the same time.”

Then, deliberately lighter, he reached for the next book. “Alright,” he said, opening it. “Before we accidentally solve drow courtship instead of Kalen Fiends—” a glance, amused “—let’s get back to mysterious external intelligences and academic despair.”

Auri listened as Redd spoke, fingers tracing the edge of the book. By the time he’d finished, the knot in her chest had loosened. “You’re right, and I will talk to him about all of it…when it’s the right moment.”

Then he shifted the focus back on studying, and a small smile tugged at her mouth. “Right. Back to Kalen Fiends and their mysterious external intelligences,” she said, rolling her shoulders and drawing the text back over. “You know—- *the simple stuff.*”

The two spent the remainder of their time together researching, with the occasional off-task discussion or joke. When it was time to step away, she pulled him into a brief, warm embrace—- if he allowed it—- before parting.

“Seriously, Redd. Thank you,” she said. “Remind me that I owe you some sound advice at some point.”
